topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed, a grating soundtrack to Amelia’s mounting frustration. She tapped her pen against the screen, the rhythmic click sounding sharp in the otherwise quiet study carrel. Another hour spent staring at the same blank page, another cycle of tweaking, revising, deleting. "Come on," she muttered under her breath, the sound barely audible above the distant keyboard clatter. She’d poured weeks into this narrative, a genuine glimpse into her soul, and now it felt stagnant, trapped behind a wall of self-doubt. Then, tucked between two unrelated admissions profiles, she saw it: a nearly identical essay, perfectly polished, with a glowing recommendation attached. A cold knot tightened in her stomach. She snatched the printout, her fingers trembling slightly as she scanned the flawless prose. The sheer ease of it made her jaw clench. Why had this slipped through? She felt a sudden, sharp urge to tear the paper up, to just move on, to stop analyzing and start *doing*.

[story 2]

Mark paced the length of his cramped dorm room, the worn carpet muffling his hurried footsteps. He kept checking his email, his thumb hovering over the refresh button with increasing anxiety. The deadline loomed like a physical weight pressing down on his chest. He’d spent the last forty-eight hours wrestling with the opening paragraph, trying to find the perfect hook that captured the essence of his experience, but every attempt felt clumsy, inadequate. He stopped abruptly, running a hand through his already messy hair. His gaze fell upon a shared drive folder he’d carelessly left open. There, glaringly obvious, was the essay he’d painstakingly crafted—or so he thought. It was sitting there, published, formatted, waiting for someone else’s acceptance. A surge of hot, frustrated energy flooded his system. He snatched the file, his breath catching in his throat. He didn't bother reading the rest; he just needed to know *why* it was so perfect, and then he needed to figure out how to make his own version match that impossible standard, immediately.

[story 3]

Sarah leaned back in her chair, the springs groaning in protest beneath her. The clock on the wall seemed to mock her with its steady, unyielding tick. She had proofread the essay three times, corrected every comma, polished every anecdote, yet the feeling persisted—a restless energy buzzing just beneath her skin, demanding release. She sighed, a long, drawn-out sound that carried the weight of weeks of effort. As she scrolled through the final submission portal, she noticed a link embedded within a supplementary materials section. Curiosity, sharp and unwelcome, pulled her down. It was an example essay, clearly marked. Her eyes devoured the words, and a wave of intense dissatisfaction washed over her. It was too smooth, too confident. She felt a sudden, almost physical urge to slam her laptop shut, to abandon the entire process and just walk out into the noise, anything to escape the suffocating pressure of needing to be flawless right now.
